book.title: Khalsa Kreatives: A Sikh & Panjabi Cultural Colouring Book
book.subtitle: Celebrating Heritage, History & Values Through Art
book.author: Khalsa Kreatives

format.interior: Black & white on white 60# uncoated paper

products.name: Khalsa Kreatives Digital Colouring Book
products.deliverable: PDF download via time-expiring secure link

products.name: Khalsa Kreatives Physical Colouring Book
products.deliverable: Printed book via Lulu Express POD

free_sample.description: 3-page sample pack used for lead generation via chatbot

pages.title: The Khanda
pages.theme: The central symbol of Sikhi representing divine knowledge, spiritual and temporal power
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, single bold outline illustration of the Sikh Khanda symbol at center, surrounded by an ornate circular mandala border with lotus petals and geometric patterns radiating outward, thick clean lines suitable for children to color, no shading no gradients no fill, pure vector line art on white background, 8.5x11 inch portrait layout, professional coloring book quality, symmetrical design
pages.educational_text: The Khanda is the emblem of the Sikh faith. The double-edged sword in the centre represents divine knowledge. The circle (Chakkar) symbolises God's eternal nature. The two curved swords (Kirpans) represent Miri and Piri — worldly and spiritual power.

pages.title: The Ik Onkar
pages.theme: The opening words of Guru Granth Sahib — there is One Universal Creator
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, large ornamental Ik Onkar symbol in Gurmukhi script at centre, embellished with flowing paisley patterns and delicate floral vines, surrounded by a decorative arch frame inspired by Sikh gurdwara architecture with scalloped edges and geometric tile patterns, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait, professional coloring book style
pages.educational_text: Ik Onkar means 'There is One God.' These are the opening words of the Mool Mantar, the foundational prayer in Guru Granth Sahib Ji. It teaches that the Creator is universal, without form, and present in all of creation.

pages.title: The Golden Temple — Harmandir Sahib
pages.theme: The holiest gurdwara in Sikhi, Amritsar, surrounded by the sacred pool
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, detailed architectural outline of Harmandir Sahib (Golden Temple) viewed from the front across the Sarovar (sacred pool), showing the ornate dome, arched windows, and walkway bridge, water with gentle ripple line patterns, decorative border of marigold garlands and lotus flowers along page edges, thick clean lines no shading no fill no gradients, pure vector line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: Harmandir Sahib, known as the Golden Temple, sits in the holy city of Amritsar, Panjab. Built by Guru Arjan Dev Ji, it has four doors — one on each side — welcoming people of all backgrounds. The Langar hall serves free meals to over 100,000 visitors every day.

pages.title: Guru Nanak Dev Ji
pages.theme: The founder of Sikhi, spreading the message of equality and oneness
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, serene portrait outline of Guru Nanak Dev Ji sitting cross-legged in meditation posture, wearing a simple robe and turban with a halo of radiant lines behind the head, one hand raised in blessing, Bhai Mardana Ji sitting beside playing the Rabab instrument, surrounded by a nature scene with rolling hills and a rising sun, decorative paisley border frame, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait, professional coloring book style
pages.educational_text: Guru Nanak Dev Ji (1469–1539) is the founder of Sikhi. He travelled thousands of miles on foot across Asia, teaching that all people are equal regardless of caste, religion, or gender. His companion Bhai Mardana Ji accompanied him, playing sacred music.

gurmukhi_vocab.english: One God, whose name is Truth

pages.title: Guru Gobind Singh Ji & the Panj Piare
pages.theme: The tenth Guru and the creation of the Khalsa in 1699
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, dynamic scene outline of Guru Gobind Singh Ji standing tall in warrior attire with a plumed turban and two kirpans, addressing a gathering, five figures (Panj Piare) kneeling before him in devotion, the Nishan Sahib flag flying in background, decorative border of crossed swords and chakrams with floral corner medallions, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: In 1699 at Anandpur Sahib, Guru Gobind Singh Ji asked who would give their life for their faith. Five brave Sikhs stepped forward — they became the Panj Piare (Five Beloved Ones). Guru Ji created the Khalsa, a community of saint-soldiers committed to justice and equality.

pages.title: Guru Granth Sahib Ji
pages.theme: The eternal living Guru of the Sikhs — the holy scripture
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, respectful illustration outline of Guru Granth Sahib Ji placed on a decorated Palki (throne) under an ornate canopy with draped fabric and tassels, a Chaur Sahib (ceremonial whisk) being waved above, Rumala cloths with intricate embroidery patterns draped over the scripture, decorative border of Gurmukhi letterforms and floral scrollwork, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait, professional coloring book style
pages.educational_text: Guru Granth Sahib Ji is the eternal Guru of the Sikhs. It contains 1,430 pages of sacred poetry and hymns composed by six Sikh Gurus and saints from Hindu, Muslim, and other traditions. Guru Gobind Singh Ji declared it the living Guru for all time.

pages.title: Seva — Selfless Service
pages.theme: The Sikh principle of serving others without expecting anything in return
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, warm scene outline of a diverse group of people in a Langar hall kitchen, adults and children working together cooking in large pots and serving food on trays, one figure kneeling to wash dishes, another carrying a stack of chapattis, patterned tile floor and brick walls with hanging utensils, decorative border of wheat stalks and cooking vessels, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: Seva means selfless service. In every Gurdwara, volunteers cook, clean, and serve free meals (Langar) to anyone who comes — regardless of their background. Seva teaches humility, compassion, and the joy of helping others without expecting reward.

pages.title: Chardi Kala — Eternal Optimism
pages.theme: The Sikh spirit of relentless positivity and resilience
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, uplifting illustration outline of a smiling Sikh child standing on a hilltop with arms raised toward a radiant sunrise, wearing a patka (children's turban), birds soaring in the sky, rolling Panjabi farmland landscape below with mustard fields and a distant village, decorative border of swirling clouds and sunbeams with geometric corner accents, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait, professional coloring book style
pages.educational_text: Chardi Kala means 'ever-rising spirit.' It is the Sikh practice of maintaining optimism, courage, and joy even during life's hardest moments. Sikhs end their daily Ardas (prayer) by asking Waheguru for Chardi Kala for all of humanity.

pages.title: Miri Piri — Spiritual & Worldly Balance
pages.theme: The dual swords representing spiritual devotion and social responsibility
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, symbolic illustration outline of two ornate crossed Kirpans (swords) in the centre representing Miri and Piri, one sword wrapped in a prayer bead mala (spiritual), the other adorned with a shield and armour motifs (temporal), surrounded by a balanced mandala with half showing meditation imagery (lotus flowers, flames) and half showing community imagery (hands serving, building), decorative border with intertwined vines and geometric patterns, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout
pages.educational_text: Miri Piri means worldly authority and spiritual authority. Guru Hargobind Ji wore two swords to show that Sikhs must be both devoted in prayer and active in protecting the weak. A Sikh is a saint-soldier: compassionate in spirit and courageous in action.

pages.title: Sarbat Da Bhala — Welfare of All
pages.theme: The Sikh prayer for the wellbeing of all humanity
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, heartfelt scene outline of children from diverse backgrounds holding hands in a circle around a globe of the Earth, each child wearing different cultural clothing, Sikh child in dastar at centre, flowers and doves surrounding the circle, decorative border of linked hands and peace symbols with Gurmukhi script accents, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait, professional coloring book style
pages.educational_text: Every Sikh prayer ends with 'Nanak Naam Chardi Kala, Tere Bhane Sarbat Da Bhala' — 'With your blessing, may there be prosperity for all.' Sikhs believe in working for the good of every person on Earth, not just their own community.

pages.title: Kesh — Uncut Hair
pages.theme: Respecting the natural form God has given, crowned with the Dastar (turban)
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, proud portrait outline of a Sikh person tying a beautiful Dastar (turban), showing the long flowing Kesh (hair) being carefully wrapped, multiple turban styles shown as smaller vignettes around the main image (Patiala Shahi, Dumalla, Patka for children), decorative border of flowing fabric folds and ornamental pins (turban pins), thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: Kesh (uncut hair) is the first of the Five Ks. Sikhs keep their hair unshorn as a gift from Waheguru, respecting the body's natural form. The Dastar (turban) is worn with pride to cover and protect the Kesh — it represents sovereignty, honour, and self-respect.

pages.title: The Five Kakars
pages.theme: The five articles of faith worn by initiated Sikhs
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, five distinct objects arranged in a star pattern at centre: Kesh (flowing hair with a Kangha comb in it), Kara (steel bracelet), Kirpan (small ceremonial sword in sheath), Kachera (traditional undergarment shown respectfully folded), each item drawn with fine detail and labeled, surrounded by a decorative circular Chakkar frame with radiating geometric patterns, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: The Five Ks (Panj Kakars) are articles of faith: Kesh (uncut hair), Kangha (wooden comb — discipline), Kara (steel bracelet — bond with God), Kachera (cotton undergarment — self-control), and Kirpan (ceremonial sword — duty to protect the weak).

pages.title: Vaisakhi — The Harvest Festival
pages.theme: Celebrating Vaisakhi, the Panjabi new year and birth of the Khalsa
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, joyful celebration scene outline of Vaisakhi festival, people performing Bhangra and Giddha dances in traditional Panjabi clothing (kurta pajama, phulkari dupatta, ghagra), dhol drummer in centre, Nishan Sahib flag and bunting decorations, wheat harvest sheaves in foreground, decorative border of wheat ears and marigold flowers, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: Vaisakhi is celebrated on April 13th or 14th each year. It marks the Panjabi harvest festival and, for Sikhs, the birth of the Khalsa in 1699. Communities celebrate with Nagar Kirtan (street processions), Bhangra dancing, and sharing festive foods.

pages.title: The Gurdwara
pages.theme: The Sikh place of worship — open to all
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, welcoming exterior outline of a beautiful Gurdwara building with distinctive domed architecture, four open doorways visible, Nishan Sahib (Sikh flag on tall flagpole) flying outside, families of diverse backgrounds walking up the entrance steps, shoe rack at the entrance, decorative border of architectural arches and jali lattice patterns, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: A Gurdwara ('Gateway to the Guru') is the Sikh place of worship. It has four doors to show it is open to people of every direction and background. Inside, the Guru Granth Sahib Ji is read, Kirtan (hymns) are sung, and everyone shares Langar together.

pages.title: Phulkari — Flower Embroidery
pages.theme: The traditional Panjabi art of floral embroidery on textiles
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, full-page geometric Phulkari embroidery pattern design featuring traditional motifs: repeated diamond shapes filled with star patterns, peacocks, wheat stalks, and stylized flowers arranged in a symmetrical grid, border of traditional Panjabi textile trim patterns, designed to look like a fabric swatch ready to be colored in vibrant colors, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: Phulkari means 'flower work.' It is the traditional embroidery art of Panjab, created by women using bright silk threads on handspun cotton cloth. Each region of Panjab has its own patterns. A Bagh ('garden') is a Phulkari so densely stitched that no base fabric shows through.

pages.title: Panjabi Alphabet — Gurmukhi
pages.theme: The sacred script created by Guru Angad Dev Ji to write the Panjabi language
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, the full Gurmukhi alphabet arranged in a beautiful grid layout, each of the 35 letters drawn in large ornamental outlined font style with decorative flourishes, small illustration icons next to key letters showing objects that start with that sound (like a children's alphabet poster), decorative border of open books and ink pots with quill pens, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: Gurmukhi means 'from the mouth of the Guru.' This script was standardised by Guru Angad Dev Ji so that everyone could read and write, regardless of caste. The Guru Granth Sahib Ji is written in Gurmukhi. The alphabet has 35 letters and is written left to right.

gurmukhi_vocab.english: From the Guru's mouth / Panjabi script

pages.title: Mai Bhago — The Warrior Saint
pages.theme: The legendary Sikh woman warrior who led soldiers in battle
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, powerful portrait outline of Mai Bhago riding a horse in full warrior armour, holding a spear aloft, flowing dupatta and turban trailing in the wind, horse in mid-gallop with detailed mane and tail, dust clouds at hooves, background showing the battlefield of Muktsar with distant fortifications, decorative border of shields and spears with rose and lotus flower accents, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: Mai Bhago was a courageous Sikh warrior who led 40 soldiers back into battle at Muktsar in 1705. She fought alongside Guru Gobind Singh Ji and survived the battle. She is remembered as a symbol of bravery and a reminder that Sikh women have always been warriors and leaders.

pages.title: The Sahibzade — Sons of Guru Gobind Singh Ji
pages.theme: Honouring the four young sons who gave their lives for their faith
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, solemn and respectful illustration outline of four young boys standing together in traditional Sikh attire with small turbans and kamar-kasa sashes, the two elder boys (Ajit Singh and Jujhar Singh) standing protectively behind the two younger boys (Zorawar Singh and Fateh Singh), a radiant Khanda symbol glowing above them, fortress walls of Anandpur Sahib in background, decorative border of eternal flames and lotus flowers, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: The Sahibzade are the four sons of Guru Gobind Singh Ji. Sahibzada Ajit Singh (18) and Jujhar Singh (14) fought bravely in battle. Sahibzada Zorawar Singh (9) and Fateh Singh (6) chose death over giving up their faith. Their courage inspires Sikhs to stand firm in their beliefs.

gurmukhi_vocab.english: Revered sons / princes of the Guru

pages.title: Sikh Mandala — Sacred Geometry
pages.theme: A meditative geometric pattern incorporating Sikh symbols
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, intricate full-page mandala design incorporating Sikh symbols: Khanda at the absolute centre, radiating outward with rings of Ik Onkar symbols, Chakkar circles, lotus petals, paisley teardrops, geometric star patterns, and ornate floral scrollwork, outermost ring featuring tiny repeated Nishan Sahib flags, extremely detailed symmetrical design suitable for older children and adults, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: Mandalas represent the universe in sacred art traditions. This mandala weaves together symbols of Sikhi into a pattern for meditation and reflection. As you colour each ring, reflect on the values they represent: truth, compassion, contentment, humility, and love.

pages.title: Waheguru — Colour Your Blessing
pages.theme: A final celebratory page with the word Waheguru as the centrepiece
pages.ai_prompt: Clean black-and-white coloring book page, the word Waheguru written in large ornamental Gurmukhi calligraphy as the centrepiece, each letter elaborately decorated with internal floral and geometric patterns like illuminated manuscript lettering, surrounded by radiating sunburst lines and floating flower petals, small vignettes in each corner showing: a child meditating, hands in seva, a blooming lotus, and an open Guru Granth Sahib, decorative border of interlinked infinity loops and stars, thick bold outlines no shading no fill, pure line art on white background, 8.5x11 portrait layout, professional coloring book quality
pages.educational_text: Waheguru means 'Wonderful Lord' — it is the Sikh name for God. Sikhs repeat Waheguru in meditation (Simran) to feel connected to the divine presence in everything. You have now journeyed through the beauty of Sikh heritage. Waheguru Ji Ka Khalsa, Waheguru Ji Ki Fateh!
